coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots decorated on chromium oxide and zirconium metal-organic framework composite (GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3) for asymmetric supercapacitors and hydrogen production
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Hassan, Haseebul
Shoaib, Misbah
khan, khakemin
Ghanem, Mohamed A.
Osman, Makkawi
coredata.dc:description: The supercapattery is a cutting-edge energy storage technology that combines the higher energy density of batteries with the superior power density and rate capability of supercapacitors. A new class of porous materials known as metal organic framework (MOF) is produced by the robust connections that form between metal ions and organic linkers. MOFs can exhibit exceptional chemical stability, a huge pore volume, and a very high surface area by carefully selecting their constituents. In this article, we present a hybrid composite of chromium oxide and zirconium metal organic framework embedded with graphene quantum dots (GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3) composite. In asymmetric supercapacitor configuration (GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3//A.C), the hybrid device reached a maximum Qs of 247C/g at 1.0 A/g. The GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3//A.C device reached a noteworthy power density of 850. W/kg and a maximum energy density of 61.44 Wh/kg. The device can exhibits remarkable 87 % cycle stability after 10,000 cycles. The hybrid material is appropriate and prospective for use in industrial settings as a supercapattery electrode material because of these remarkable properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Energy storage device
GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3
Electrochemical performance
Metal–organic frameworks

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–c) SEM images for Zr-MOF, Cr2O3, and GQDs@Zr-MOF@Cr2O3. (d) XRD study for Zr-MOF, Cr2O3, and GQDs@Zr-MOF@Cr2O3. (e) FTIR study for Zr-MOF, Cr2O3, and GQDs@Zr-MOF@Cr2O3. (f) TGA study for Zr-MOF, Cr2O3, and GQDs@Zr-MOF@Cr2O3. (g) BET analysis for GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3
Deconvoluted XPS spectrum for (a) C 1s, (b) N 1s, (c) Cr 2p, (d) Zr 3d, and (e) O 1s.

(a) EIS analysis for GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3. (b) CV analysis for GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3. (c) GCD analysis for GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3. (d) ESR for all three composites values obtained from EIS graph. (e) Qs calculated through CV at 3 mV/s for all three samples. (f) Qs attained through GCD at 1.0 A/gfor all three samples. (g) Qs calculated through CV at various scan for all three samples. (f) Qs attained through GCD at various current density for all three samples. (i) Capacity retention after 15,000 cycles.
(a) Systematic diagram of GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3//AC device. (b) CV comparison of capacitive and battery electrode in a three electrode setup. (c, d) CV and GCD analysis of GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3//AC.

(a) Stability evaluation for GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3//AC device after 10,000 cycles. (b, c) Qs Calculated for GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3//AC device and compared it with Zr-MOF//AC and Cr2O3//AC device through CV and GCD. (d) b-value attained for GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3//AC device at various potential. (e) Ed and Pd attained for GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3//AC device.

(a) LSV curve for Pt/C, Zr-MOF, Cr2O3, and GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3. (b) Overpotential measurements for Pt/C, Zr-MOF, Cr2O3, and GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3. (c) Tafel slope for Pt/C, Zr-MOF, Cr2O3, and GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3. (d) LSV curve for GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3 before and after stability test.
Specific capacity and capacitance for Cr2O3, Zr-MOF and GQDs@Cr2O3/Zr-MOF composite calculated through CV and GCD.
Stability presentation evaluation for GQDs@Cr2O3/Zr-MOF//AC composite after 9000 cycles.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Ed and Pd evaluation used for GQDs@Cr2O3/Zr-MOF//AC device.
Tafel slope and overpotential comparison for GQDs@Cr2O3/Zr-MOF.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots decorated on chromium oxide and zirconium metal-organic framework composite (GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3) for asymmetric supercapacitors and hydrogen production

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Riphah International University, Campus Lahore, Pakistan

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Liwa College Faculty of Engineering Mechanical and Industrial Department United Arab Emirates, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The supercapattery is a cutting-edge energy storage technology that combines the higher energy density of batteries with the superior power density and rate capability of supercapacitors. A new class of porous materials known as metal organic framework (MOF) is produced by the robust connections that form between metal ions and organic linkers. MOFs can exhibit exceptional chemical stability, a huge pore volume, and a very high surface area by carefully selecting their constituents. In this article, we present a hybrid composite of chromium oxide and zirconium metal organic framework embedded with graphene quantum dots (GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3) composite. In asymmetric supercapacitor configuration (GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3//A.C), the hybrid device reached a maximum Qs of 247C/g at 1.0 A/g. The GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3//A.C device reached a noteworthy power density of 850. W/kg and a maximum energy density of 61.44 Wh/kg. The device can exhibits remarkable 87 % cycle stability after 10,000 cycles. The hybrid material is appropriate and prospective for use in industrial settings as a supercapattery electrode material because of these remarkable properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The need for eco-friendly, efficient, and clean energy harvesting and storage systems is increasing due to continued technological breakthroughs and the rising emissions from fuel waste, which are posing serious environmental concerns [1,2]. The need for energy has increased recently due to the development of wearable electronics, the dissemination of portable tools, and the rapid expansion of the electric vehicle market. The current concentration of field is on controlling the production and consumption of green energy, but the next generation of smart electric grids will require inexpensive, safe energy storage technologies due to population expansion. Because of their great endurance, high energy and power densities, and combination of both, electrochemical energy storage systems have attracted a lot of interest [3–5]. The best options for energy storage devices are Li-ion batteries because of their extended lifespan and high gravimetric energy density (260 Wh/kg). However, careful resource management is required due to the uneven distribution of lithium deposits throughout the world and their scarcity [6]. In order to facilitate the diminution of microelectronics and the growing Internet of Things for diverse energy applications, scientists are investigating alternate energy storage techniques [7]. In this environment, supercapacitors show great promise as fast storage of energy and high-power densities [8–13]. Compared to high-energy storage batteries, supercapacitors have a longer cycle life and faster charge-discharge operations. The charge storage mechanism is what determines the performance of supercapacitors. In this process, charge carriers build either at the electrode surface through reversible redox processes (pseudocapacitors or PCs) or through electrolyte ion absorption/desorption (electric double-layer capacitors, or EDLCs) [14]. The properties of both kinds of charge storage techniques can be combined to produce asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC), a device made up of two different nature electrode (batteries and supercapacitors) [15,16]. The performance of the device is greatly influenced by the electrode material in addition to the charge storage mechanism. For a ASC, the perfect positive electrode material should be porous, highly conductible, and electroactive [17,18]. The performance of ASCs is determined by two important factors: electrodes and electrolytes. The inherent characteristics of the electrode material have a significant impact on the reaction kinetics. As such, the search for novel electrode materials is an ongoing project [19–24]. The physical and chemical qualities associated with the structural features of nanoparticles are intended to be used in a multitude of applications [25,26].

Metal organic frameworks (MOFs) have enormous surface areas, uniform distributions of metal sites, rich and tunable components, and tunable optical absorption abilities. The development of chemically resilient MOFs becomes essential to further extend the usage of MOFs for catalysis and similar applications performed in aqueous solutions, as the bulk of currently available MOFs still exhibit poor water stability [27]. Zirconium-based MOFs (Zr-MOFs) and their hafnium-based analogs are well-known for having outstanding chemical and thermal stability, among the many MOFs that have been discovered thus far. Several Zr-MOFs have been shown to demonstrate outstanding durability in water and acidic solutions, steam, and even H2S vapor. Because of their exceptional chemicastability, Zr-MOFs may be used in a variety of wet or humid conditions [28].

Transition metal oxides (TMOs) have been thoroughly studied as electrode materials for SCs applications recently. Metal oxides may be utilized as pseudocapacitance materials for developing SCs since they contain several oxidation states that result in various redox processes. As a cheap transition metal oxide, Cr2O3 has found recent applications in a wide range of sectors, such as supercapacitors [29,30], Li batteries [31], sensors [32], and catalysts [33]. For the invention of supercapacitor applications, chromium compounds having a Cr2+/Cr3+ redox pair are a viable option. Unfortunately, because of the large volume changes that occur during charge-discharge operations, Cr2O3 often has low cycle stability, which makes it impractical for use in supercapacitors [34]. Making a composite with MOF based materials is an alternate method to enhance the cycling performance of Cr2O3. Because it can limit volume change of Cr2O3 during cycling operations. Nadeem et al. [35] developed Cr2O3 carbon porous nanocomposites. The resultant nanocomposite showed a capacitance preservation of 95.5 % after 3000 cycles and a capacitance of 291 F/g.

In the current work, we report a novel combination of chromium based oxide with zirconium metal organic framework. Additionally, to enhance the surface area and electrical conductance 10 % GQDs were added in this composite. Till now, there is no report which effectively describe the application of GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3 (GZC) in energy storage and electrocatalytic applications. The produced GZC electrode was utilized in both three (half) and two (full) electrode design. The catalytic application of GZC electrode was evaluated in hydrogen evolution reaction.

Chromium (III) nitrate nonahydrate (Cr(NO3)3·9H2O and Poly (vinyl pyrrolidone) (PVP, (C6H9NO)n supplied by Sigma-Aldrich (USA) and they have been used without pre-treatment or further purification. Zirconium (IV) chloride (ZrCl4), terephthalic acid (H2BDC), were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.

Measure 2.45 g of ZrCl₄ (zirconium chloride) and dissolve it in 20 mL of deionized water (DIW), stirring until completely dissolved. Separately, measure 2.67 g of terephthalic acid (H₂BDC) and dissolve it in 40 mL of N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), mixing until fully dissolved. Combine the zirconium solution with the organic linker solution, and add a modulator (such as acetic acid or formic acid) to regulate MOF crystal nucleation and growth. Transfer the combined solution to a stainless steel autoclave lined with Teflon and securely close the lid. Place the autoclave in an oven and heat it to a temperature between 175 °C for a duration of 12h. After the heating period, allow the autoclave to cool naturally to room temperature before carefully opening it. Collect the Zr-MOF crystals, the solid product, and clean them by heating under vacuum at high temperatures (120 °C) to remove any unreacted components.

First, 0.8 M solutions of chromium nitrate and sodium hydroxide were made in order to synthesize chromium oxide. First we separately dissolve 0.93 g of chromium nitrate and 1.2799 g sodium hydroxide into 20 ml of DI water to make two different solutions. Each of these solutions was stirred for 30 min separately to ensure complete homogenization. While stirring, the sodium hydroxide solution was combined with the chromium nitrate solution drop by drop. The obtained solution was mixed once more for 30 min to achieve homogeneity following the complete mixing of the solutions. After filling an autoclave with this uniform solution, the autoclave was heated to 160 °C for 10h. After adding DIW to the obtained solution, it was centrifuged to remove contaminants and adjust the pH. To eliminate any leftover contaminants and DIW with ethanol, this washing process was repeated. The resultant precipitates were then crushed into a fine powder and dried for 6 h at 70 °C in a furnace.

To synthesize the Cr2O3/Zr-MOF heterostructure material, the hydrothermal method was applied to previously synthesized Cr2O3 and Zr-MOF. Cr2O3 and Zr-MOF were first mixed in a 50/50 wt ratio, and the resulting mixture was subsequently dissolved in a DMF solvent. Blending was used to fully combine the final product. After that, the mixture was placed in a Teflon autoclave and heated to 150oC for 8h. The previously steps are followed to attain Zr-MOF/Cr2O3 composite. A 10 % GQDs also added in the Zr-MOF/Cr2O3 composite and name the final material as GZC. Schematic representation for the synthesis of GZC material was presented in Fig. S1.

The electrode was prepared by depositing GZC material on nickel foam (NF) substrate. The electrochemical performance of each sample was examined using galvanostatic charge-discharge and cyclic voltammetry measurements. 75 % weight of active material Cr2O3, Zr-MOF and GQDs@ Cr2O3/Zr-MOF, 15 % carbon black, and 10 % PVDF binder were mixed to produce the electrode material slurry. Using a micropipette, the homogenous slurry was deposited. The produced slurry was applied to a 1 × 1 surface. A total of 5 mg of the GZC material was deposited. After 6h of drying at 60 °C, the working electrodes were put to use for electrochemical testing. Electrodes were tested in 1 M KOH electrolyte. Platinum wire was used as the counter electrode while Hg/HgO served as the reference electrode in a three-electrode assembly. The weight of the active material was in between 5 and 7 mg for each composite.

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis was used to study the morphological characteristics for the Zr-MOF, Cr₂O₃, and GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr₂O₃ (Fig. 1 (a-c)). The SEM image of Zr-MOF, which is representative of its metal-organic framework nature, is presented in Fig. 1(a). It is distinguished by clearly defined crystalline structures and a highly porous surface. The SEM image of Cr2O₃, which is representative of chromium oxide nanoparticles, shows aggregated particles with a granulated and rough surface roughness (Fig. 1(b)). The GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr₂O₃ composite's SEM image in Fig. 1(c) shows a more intricate and entwined morphology, fusing the granular characteristics of Cr₂O₃ with the porous structure of Zr-MOF and indicating the successful incorporation of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) into the composite matrix. The hybrid composite exhibits increased heterogeneity and surface roughness, which is probably going to help with improved electrochemical efficiency in hydrogen generation and ASCs applications.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to determine the crystal structure of the samples at 30 kV using Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Ao) as represented in Fig. 1(d). XRD patterns of Zr MOF, with different peaks at 2θ = 3.1°, 4.9°, 20.0°, 38.4° and 48.2° could be indexed to the (111), (002), (004), (224) and (137) crystal planes, respectively, of the Zr MOF phase structure [36]. The Cr2O3 XRD pattern corresponded precisely with ICSD file No. 00-038-1479. The presence of multiple diffraction peaks of (012), (104), (110), (113), (024), (116), (018), (214), (030), (1010), and (220) at the angle of 5.4°, 20.2°, 30.1°, 40.4°, 50.1°, 55.2°, 64.2°, 70.1°, 75.3°, 81.0°, and 87.5°respectively in the diffraction patterns suggests that the Cr2O3 sample displays a characteristic hexagonal structure [37]. There were no peaks in the pattern indicating impurities. And the doping material GQDs have oly one crystal plane (002) at the angle of 25.0° [38].

The FTIR analysis for Zr-MOF, Cr2O3, and GQDs@ Cr2O3/Zr-MOF were represented in Fig. 1(e). The Zr-MOF FT-IR spectra displays a broad absorption peak (at roughly 3433 cm−1) associated with the O–H and N–H stretching modes, which are symmetric and asymmetric respectively. The intense doublet at 1572 and 1386 cm−1 are attributed to the asymmetrical and symmetrical stretching modes of the carboxylate groups (two tightly linked C–O bonds with bond strengths intermediate between CO and C–O), whereas the peak at 1654 cm−1 is attributed to DMF. At 1258 cm−1, the strong aromatic C–N stretching band is detected. The peaks that are detected between 1400 and 1500 cm−1 are attributed to the aromatic compound CC organic connector. The aromatic ring C–C vibrational mode is attributed to the peak located at 769 cm−1 (Fig. 2 ). Cr2O3 NPs FTIR spectra in the 4000–400 cm−1 range showed the distinctive bands at 3448, 1636, 649, and 582 cm−1. The O–H stretching of the surface hydroxyl groups and undissociated water molecules is what causes the band at 3448 cm−1. It is reasonable to attribute the absorption at 1636 cm−1 to the bending modes of undissociated water molecules. There were strong bands at 649 and 582 cm−1, which suggested the existence of crystalline α-Cr2O3.

The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) experiments up to 800 °C for Zr-MOF, Cr2O3, and GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3 was performed Fig. 1(f). Using a TG experiment, the termal stability of Zr-MOF particles was examined. The as-synthesized Zr-MOF sample is thermally stable for up to 210 °C. At temperatures below 100 °C, the initial 4.5 % weight loss is ascribed to the elimination of water molecules. The loss of FA and DMF as guest molecules from the pores of Zr-MOF is thought to be the cause of the second weight loss, which started at about 210 °C. It is possible that the breakdown of organic linkers and the collapse of MOF are the causes of the significant weight loss that occurs at about 280 °C. Using TG curves, the carbon content of the products was further determined. The trials were conducted in air, with a heating rate of 10 C min−1 from ambient temperature to 800 C. As demonstrated in line, carbon concentration is 7.2 % for Cr2O3. A portion of the glycine has broken down to produce the carbon in the Cr2O3. The carbon that is added to the Cr2O3 comes from the carbon in glucose. The composite GZC showed enhanced thermal stability, which is beneficial for energy storage and catalytic applications.

Using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method, the surface area, pore volume, and pore size distribution for Zr-MOF, Cr2O3, and GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3 (GZC) materials were determined. Through BET analysis, the textural data, such as specific surface area, pore volume, and pore size distribution, are compiled to validate the impact of pore structure on catalytic performance. The BET surface study of Zr-MOF is performed to look into the surface area, pore-volume, and pore diameter. Based on the BET plot, the Zr-MOF surface area is determined to be 1457 m2/g (Fig. S2). It is discovered that the Zr-MOF has a total pore volume of about 0.6 cm3/g. As anticipated, catalyst Cr2O3 has a specific surface area of only 387 m2/g and pore volume 0.4 cm3/g (Fig. S2). A larger surface area is advantageous for supercapacitor applications as it provides more active charge storage sites, enhancing capacitance. BET surface area testing revealed a surface area of 1895 m2/g, pore volume of about 0.9 cm3/g (Fig. 1(g)). Furthermore, the pore diameters for Zr-MOF, Cr2O3, and GQDs@Cr2O3/Zr-MOF were found to be 0.21 nm, 0.18 nm, and 0.29 nm, respectively. The accessibility and distribution of electrolyte ions within the material are directly impacted by pore size. Ion diffusion is maximized and efficient ion transport is ensured by an ideal pore size.

The elemental composition, chemical state, and electronic state of materials can all be precisely determined with XPS. Peaks at 284.2, 285.4, 287.6, and 289.2 eV are seen in the high-resolution spectrum of C 1s; these values correspond to CC, C–C, C–O, and CO (Fig. 2(a)). The peak for C–NH2 in l-Tyrosine can be seen at 400.4 eV in the N 1s high-resolution spectra (Fig. 2(b)). Cr 2p electron binding energy values were found to be located at 586.4 ev for Cr 2p1/2, and 577 eV for Cr 2p3/2 respectively (Fig. 2(c)). The lower binding energy peak located at 577 eV is assigned to the Cr3+ state. The results indicate that despite the fact that the calcination products are essentially α-Cr2O3, the surface is partially oxidized to expose Cr ≥ 3+ ions.

A prominent peak is seen at 187.6 eV (Zr 3d5/2) in the high-resolution spectra of Zr 3d, corresponding to Zr–O. Another peak at 183.4 eV (Zr 3d3/2) corresponds to Zr–O. This suggests the presence of the Zr–O bond, in which Zr (II) ion connects with ligand via O-atoms (Fig. 2(d)). XRD results revealed that the four oxygen atoms bond with a single Zr-ion in the tetragonal phase, which causes the binding energy of Zr with O to shift to 187.6 eV [39]. The earlier study also provided evidence in favor of Zr binding to O atoms [40]. The O 1s spectrum represented in Fig. 2(e) indicates the peak at 528.3 belongs to O2⁻ ions in Cr₂O₃ and ZrO₂ while the peak at 532.5 eV belongs to C–O (hydroxyl groups) or CO (carbonyl groups) in the MOF.

The working electrode in a three-cell was prepared using active materials (Cr2O3, Zr-MOF and GQDs@Cr2O3/Zr-MOF), counter platinum wire, and Hg/HgO as a reference. In the main manuscript, we only discuss the best material GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3 (GZC), while the electrochemical performance of individual components (Zr-MOF and Cr2O3) was discussed in the supplementary section.

Differential impedance features that are essential to comprehending the electrochemical performance of Zr-MOF, Cr2O3, and the GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3 composite are revealed by the EIS study. The EIS analysis for GZC composite was represented in Fig. 3 (a).The Nyquist plot shows an initial tiny semicircle that denotes effective ionic diffusion and low charge transfer resistance. The incorporation of GQDs improves electron transport and structural stability, which is a notable improvement in the composite's performance; nonetheless, the observed rise in Warburg impedance indicates modified diffusion processes. These results highlight the necessity of more research into the mechanisms underlying impedance change for better material design and longer battery life. The ESR values calculated for Zr-MOF, Cr2O3, and the GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3 was 1.13, 1.19, and 0.98 U, respectively (Fig. 3(d)). The EIS graph for individual components Zr-MOF and Cr2O3 was represented in Fig. S3.

The CV profiles for GZC at different scans (5, 10, 20, and 50 mV/s) are shown in Fig. 3(b). The shape of the CV curves indicates the mechanism of charge storage. The CV curves for GZC showed the strong redox peaks. Thus, the charge that was stored resulted from faradaic reactions, confirming the battery-like behavior of GZC. The integrated area also rose at increasing scans. Because of the improved material stability, the form of the CV remained unchanged even at a higher scan rate. The CV performance of Cr2O3 and Zr-MOF was represented in Fig. S4. Compared to a single Cr2O3, Zr-MOF displayed a broader region, indicating the higher efficiency of the binary GZC composite. The electrochemical activity of GZC was also evaluated using the GCD computations. GCD was carried out for the materials in a three-cell setup at OP 0–0.6V. Fig. 3(c) shows GCD curves for GZC at various currents, respectively. GCD curves displayed little humps rather than being straight or triangular. Faradaic reactions at the interface between the electrode and electrolyte are represented by these humps. These GCD curves show that the nature of GZC was the battery type, since faradaic reactions are one of the characteristics of the battery. The discharge period is shortened by a rise in current density due to increased resistance and challenging ion mobility. The GCD curves for Cr2O3 and Zr-MOF were shown in Fig. S5. Therefore, it is evident from the GCD and CV data that the best-tuned material was a GZC composite. The total charge that is retained on the electrode surface during redox reactions is known as specific capacity. CV and GCD measurements were used to determine the specific capacity for Cr2O3, Zr-MOF, and GQDs@Cr2O3/Zr-MOF by using below equations.
(1)
Q s =
∫ I d V
m v
(2)
Q s =
I × Δ t
m

The results were compared at various scan rate. At a scan rate of 5 mV/s, the estimated capacities for Zr-MOF and Cr2O3 were 1183 and 1204C/g, respectively. GZC showed the maximum storage capacity, with 1542C/g specific capacities as shown in Fig. 3(e). Similarly for GCD curves the specific capacity for Cr2O3, Zr-MOF, and GQDs@Cr2O3/Zr-MOF are shown in Fig. 3(f), at a current density of 1.0 A/g, the estimated capacities for Zr-MOF and Cr2O3 were 1243 and 1374C/g, respectively. Specific capacity of 1631C/g was achieved with GZC composite. The specific capacity at various scan rates through CV and at different current densities through GCD curves were represented in Fig. 3(g) and (h). The specific capacities for the three samples were also represented in tabular form (Table 1 ). The GZC electrode stability was also tested after 15,000 cycles. The GZC composite can retain 93 % of capacity after stability performance.

In order to develop ASCs for actual device applications, GZC was used as battery-grade material and AC as capacitive material (Fig. 4 (a)). WHATMAN paper, which acts as a semipermeable membrane to limit the flow of ions between two electrodes, was used to separate the capacitive and battery-graded electrodes. A 1 M KOH solution was made as an electrolytic solution. Prior to constructing a hybrid device, both electrodes have to be stable. Fig. 4(b) displayed the CV of each individual AC at OP of -1–0 V and the GZC at OP 0–0.6V. Due to the combined OP of AC and GZC, the asymmetric device OP is rather large, ranging from 0 to 1.6 V. Fig. 4(c) displayed the CV graph at a scan rate of 3–50 mV/s for this ASC device (GZC//AC). The graph indicates that this device exhibited fully capacitive behavior at lower potentials (0–0.6 V). Nevertheless, the behavior of this device diverged from capacitive behavior at a higher potential 0.6 V. This device behaved like a battery, with distinct redox peaks at greater voltage. Overall, the GZC//AC device validates the development of ASC since the CV plots show both capacitive and battery-type behavior. Furthermore, the CV shape did not change much even at a higher scan, demonstrating the greater stability and capacity preservation of GZC//AC. The GCD measurements were made for GZC//AC at various currents (1.0–4.0 A/g), and Fig. 4(d) displays the findings. The GCD curves showed rectangular form (capacitive behavior) along tiny humps (battery type behavior). This suggests the successful developments of ASC with GZC//AC device. The stability test is essential to figuring out how long this asymmetric device will last. In order to achieve this, 10,000 consecutive cycles of testing at a constant 5 A/g current density were performed on the hybrid device GZC//AC. Following 10,000 cycles, measurements were made and the corresponding columbic efficiency, capacity retention were displayed in Fig. 5 (a). As seen in Fig. 5(a), columbic efficiency was represented by the purple curve, while capacity retention was represented by the orange line. After 10,000 cycles, the GZC//AC displays 87 % capacity retention and 94 % columbic efficiency. The columbic efficiency and capacity retention of the GZC//AC supercapattery were compared with those of earlier studies in Table 2 . As seen in Fig. 5(b) and (c), the specific capacity for GZC//AC was 198 and 247C/g through CV and GCD. b-fitting provided additional confirmation of this device additional charge storage mechanism. Batteries have a b-value of 0–0.5, supercapacitors have a b-value of 0.8–1.0, and ASC have a b-value of 0.5–0.8 [76]. As can be seen in Fig. 5(d), the b-value measured for GZC//AC falls between 0.5 and 0.8, indicating the development of ASC.

The energy and power density for GZC//AC were calculated with the following formulas.
(3)
E =
Q × Δ V
2
X
3.6
(4)
P =
E × 3600
Δ t

These formulas yielded an energy density of 61.44 Wh/kg at 850 W/kg power density for GZC//AC (Fig. 5(e)). The comparison of GQDs@Cr2O3/Zr-MOF//AC performance with earlier research is also shown in Table 3 . The CV curves of GQDs@Cr2O3/Zr-MOF//AC were analyzed at 3 and 50 mV/s using Dunn's model, as shown in Fig. S6. An increase in scan rate improved the performance of capacitive storage. This suggests that at higher scan rates, diffusive involvement was restricted, but at lower scan rates, there was ample time for ions to interact with the electrode material.

The electrocatalytic performance for Cr2O3, Zr-MOF, and GZC were compared with standard Pt/C electrode in HER applications. The linear curve polarization patterns of Pt/C, Cr2O3, Zr-MOF, and GZC in the alkaline medium were shown in Fig. 6 (a). GZC exhibit better HER performance than Zr-MOF and Cr2O3 but lower then Pt/C at concentrations below −100 mV. The Pt/C, Zr-MOF, Cr2O3, and GZC composite electrocatalysts require over potentials of 64.23, 167.15, 125.48, and 102.31 mV against RHE in an alkaline solution as shown in Fig. 6(b) in order to achieve a current density of 10 mA/cm2. The enhanced electrocatalytic properties of the GZC are primarily due to enhanced surface area and faster movement of ions. This allows for strong bonds with the electrolyte and efficient electron transport across the link [41,42]. The electrocatalytic performance was also tested using Tafel slope as shown in Fig. 6(c). The Tafel inclination for Pt/C was 55 mV/dec and for GZC is 59.37 mV/dec. However, Zr-MOF and Cr2O3 showed greater Tafel slopes than at 65.5 and 69.2 mV/dec, respectively, indicating a faster Volmer-Heyrovsky reaction. Table 4 represents the HER performance comparison. The remarkable electrocatalytic capabilities of GZC electrocatalyst in alkaline media are determined by its low Tafel slope. The endurance of the GZC electrocatalysts was also evaluated using LSV. As can be seen in Fig. 6(d), the electrocatalytic performance only marginally declines after the stability measurements. This study demonstrates the potential of GZC heterostructures for advancing energy storage technologies and HER applications.

In this work, we used hydrothermal technique to synthesize Cr2O3, Zr-MOF and GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3 (GZC). The GZC composite outperformed the others (Cr2O3, Zr-MOF) in electrochemical tests, demonstrating a 1631C/g capacity at 1 A/g in three electrode assembly. GZC and AC were used together to build an asymmetric devic. The GZC device had a sustaining power of 850 W/kg with an energy of 61.44 Wh/kg. By subjecting GZC to 10,000 GCD cycles, its durability was evaluated. This device can hold 87 % of its capacity after 10,000 cycles. In HER applications, the GZC electrode showed and overpotential of 102.31 mV and tafel slope of 59.37 mV/dec. This study demonstrates the possible future use of GQDs@Zr-MOF/Cr2O3 in energy-storing and catalytic technologies.

Haseebul Hassan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Data curation, Conceptualization. Misbah Shoaib: Writing – original draft. khakemin khan: Software, Methodology. Mohamed A. Ghanem: Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Makkawi Osman: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
